title.en: Court Marriage Under the Special Marriage Act — Notice, 30 Days, Documents and Cost

answerBox.en: A court marriage (Special Marriage Act, 1954) works for any two adults — across religions, or anyone preferring a civil ceremony: give 30 days' written notice to the Marriage Officer of the district where either party has lived for 30+ days, and after the notice period (if no valid objection) the marriage is solemnised before the officer and three witnesses. Government fees are a few hundred rupees; the certificate issues the same day.

en.title: Check conditions and file the notice
en.summary: Age, capacity, prohibited degrees — then Form + documents.
en.detail: Conditions (Section 4, SMA): groom 21+, bride 18+, neither with a living spouse, both capable of consent, outside prohibited relationship degrees. File the **notice of intended marriage** with the **Marriage Officer** (SDM/registrar) of the district where **either** party has resided **30+ days**: passport photos, age proof (birth certificate/passport/10th certificate), address proof, affidavits of marital status (our **₹199 marriage affidavit** service), and divorce decree/death certificate where applicable. Many states accept online submission with an in-person verification visit.

en.title: The 30-day notice period — what it is and isn't
en.summary: Public notice inviting objections on legal grounds only.
en.detail: The notice is displayed at the office (and sent to the permanent-address district if different). **Objections** (Section 7) must allege a legal impediment — existing marriage, underage, prohibited degrees — and the officer inquires and decides within 30 days of the objection; rejection of frivolous objections is appealable in your favour. Family displeasure is NOT a legal objection. Couples fearing harassment: police protection can be sought (courts, following *Lata Singh*, protect adult couples' choices), and safety planning around the notice display is legitimate — take advice.

en.title: Solemnisation and the certificate
en.summary: Declarations before the officer + 3 witnesses; certificate same day.
en.detail: After 30 days (and within **3 months** of the notice, else it lapses), the marriage is **solemnised at the office** (or within reasonable distance on payment): both parties declare in the prescribed words before the **Marriage Officer and three witnesses** (any adults with ID — friends work; parents are not required). Sign the register; the **marriage certificate** issues — conclusive proof for passports, visas, banks and everything after. Total government fees typically run **₹100–1,000** by state.

en.title: Special situations
en.summary: Interfaith notes, NRI timing, and registering an existing marriage.
en.detail: **Interfaith couples**: SMA marriage requires no conversion — that is its purpose; personal-law succession consequences (Section 21/21A nuances) deserve a quick advice check. **NRIs**: the 30-day residence + 30-day notice needs calendar planning; some consulates solemnise under the Foreign Marriage Act abroad. Already married religiously? That marriage is **registered** (not re-solemnised) under state registration rules or SMA Section 15 — a different, shorter process (see our [marriage registration guide](/help/family-personal/marriage-registration)). Court marriage ≠ "instant marriage" — beware agents selling same-day certificates; those are frauds.

en.footnote: Timeline honestly stated: ~30–45 days from notice to certificate. Anyone promising a legal SMA marriage in a day is selling either a religious ceremony you'll still need to register — or a forgery.

en.q: Can the 30-day notice period be skipped?
en.a: No — it is statutory under the SMA. What varies is everything around it: online filing, appointment efficiency, and solemnisation promptly on day 31. Couples needing immediate marriage sometimes solemnise religiously first (valid under personal law) and register that marriage — different consequences; take advice for your facts.

en.q: Will my parents be informed of the notice?
en.a: The notice is displayed publicly at the marriage office, and a copy goes to the permanent-address district office if different — the law does not send personal notices to parents, but public display means discovery is possible. Adult couples' right to marry is protected; if you apprehend interference or violence, protection mechanisms exist — plan with advice, not fear.

en.q: Who can object to the marriage, and on what grounds?
en.a: Anyone may object within the 30 days, but only on legal grounds — existing spouse, age, prohibited relationship, incapacity. The officer inquires (max 30 days) and must reject non-legal objections; wrongful refusal is appealable to the district court. "We disapprove" has no legal standing.

en.q: Do witnesses have to be family members?
en.a: No — any three adults with valid ID (friends, colleagues) serve as witnesses. This matters for couples marrying without family support; no parental consent or presence is legally required for adults.

en.q: Is a court marriage certificate valid for passports and visas?
en.a: Fully — the SMA certificate is the gold-standard civil proof of marriage, accepted by passport offices, embassies and foreign registries (apostille it for use abroad). It also simplifies spouse-name changes — see our name change guide.

reviewer: Adv. Priya Sharma, Bar Council of Delhi, D/1284/2016
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
